## [00:00 - 03:01]

So this is the KPI reporting spreadsheet that we use for all of our ad accounts. So I'm going to show you how we construct it and the data that goes into building it. So the main types of ad accounts that we generally run for my own ad accounts and for clients are e-commerce and lead gen. So I'm going to show you the examples of what those sheets look like. First of all, when you get access to this sheet, hit file and make a copy so that you can work on your own version of this spreadsheet. Now the e-KPI's part, what I've tried to do here is give me an at-a-glance view of every single ad account that we run. So it doesn't matter if I'm looking at, you know, if I've only got one ad account, then this would be my view, but we run 10, 20, 30 different ad accounts and I have them all in this kind of spreadsheet format and it's super quick just to find the ad account and have a look at performance without having to go into ad manager or, you know, dashboards where you have to select drop downs and different things. So this gives me kind of almost everything I need at the top level so that I know which ad account potentially has a problem or an opportunity so that I can drill down further. Now this construct of this is based on supermetrics. So have a watch of the supermetrics video ahead of this so you know how to connect the data. The data looks like this, so I've called it raw data. This is what we pull via supermetrics into this particular tab which gives us all of the data which generates these two tabs here. So the raw data is literally just the data that comes back. So we've got the date column, which account. So for the sake of this demo, I've relabeled live accounts with just ecom ad account and lead gen ad account. But you can see I can run this sheet and run many, many ad accounts in this one raw data tab and pull them in. So for example, this ecom tab has ecom ad account. This lead gen KPIs tab has lead gen account. That matches up with the names of the accounts here. So for example, if I change the name here, it will change the data in this sheet because it references it all here. So this sheet took us a bit of time to get it right and get it operational and working. But I'm giving it to you as part of the BPM method to allow you to do the same fast data analysis. But all the formulas are set. So for example, if I delete that out, then all the formulas will break because there's no name there. But you know, equally, when you do load your data into raw data, whatever the ad account name is that appears in this column B, make sure that the name matches here. So you just copy and paste it into here, then it will

## [03:01 - 06:05]

automatically pull all the data through as long as you're pulling your data in the same order and format that you see here. So the orders important because of the references in the spreadsheets and the functions that are calling all the data, it's looking for specific columns. It's just the way that we've programmed it. So when you're pulling your data from supermetrics, make sure you go through and copy and paste down or write down the different columns that we're using. So as you go through the supermetrics training, you'll know to go into supermetrics add on and into launch, and then you can pull the data. So make sure you're pulling the same data that we have here. There aren't many columns. But this is enough for this level of analysis to tell us kind of what's going on. One thing also worth noting is when we pull data for website ads to cart, website purchases, often we'll look at the unique values as well. This really depends on what impact your kind of ad account data has on your analysis. So for example, in this live account, which I pulled the data in for this demo purpose, you can see, for example, there are differences at times with website purchase and unique website purchase. In this scenario, there's actually two scenarios where this happens. Number one, if someone makes an order or a registration or whatever your kind of thank you page or confirmation page event is, if they refresh that page, it can cause a double fire. So for that reason, using unique is good. Also, it could be that people are buying more than once in that time period as well. So for example, if someone clicked an ad on this day, and then bought, and they just happened to come back two days later and bought as well, Facebook will still reference it back to the day they made the click in your ad account. So sometimes the numbers do look off as well. So it really depends on how you want to do your analysis on that front. Then in terms of website ad to carts, we have some accounts where we have multiple ad to carts for every kind of product view. So people will add two, three, four times into their ad. And when you're looking at your cost per add to cart, as in this case, you see that the numbers are lower than they actually should be. So in this particular example, my cost per add to count on this account is, you know, give or take $3, let's say we'll round it up. But if I look at my unique add to carts, it's closer to $15. So people are adding to cart multiple times on this particular store. And that makes a difference to your analysis, because when you're writing rules, for example, and you're doing automations, or you're making decisions on which ads to keep and which ads not to keep, you could, for example, perhaps mistakenly, see a really low cost per add to

## [06:05 - 09:07]

cart and assume that the campaign or the ad set or the ad is working really, really well. But it could just be one or two users that are adding multiple items to the cart. And actually, the unique cost per add to cart is not as strong or not within your target as well. So when you're pulling that data through, you have the option to pull in website ads to cart or the unique add to carts here. And then what I would do is encourage you to pulling website purchase and unique website purchase as well. And then I'm also pulling in leads and registrations. Now, the reason I bring that all into one raw data is that when I then split it out into my lead gen and ecom KPIs, I can just focus a bit better. So for example, the data here is using exactly the same data for this ad account just for this purpose of demonstrating, obviously, it would be a separate ad account, we'll have separate spend numbers and things like that. So for example, for my agency, we're running lead gen for the agency. So this is really all I would focus on at the top level, how much have we spent? What's our cost per range? How many registrations and leads have we brought in? Then we'll go into our kind of sales data and look at things like so for example, our registration event would come in based on someone booking a calendar booking or we might use lead or something like that. So then I know what our agency lead gen is doing and how many leads are being brought through. If for example, you've got a mix of both. Now, I wouldn't necessarily split it out and kind of analyze it quite separately because here, we're looking at the spend overall for the store for this Saturday. So that's just something to bear in mind when you are doing your analysis of what else is going on in that to add accounts. So for example, if we spent 11k and generated 61 registrations, that's 195, I think it's dollars, maybe it's pounds, cost per registration, which is not great as well. ## [09:07 - 12:12]

So what I've done for this is I work on a rolling 14 day view of overall performance. It's just my preference. Some people want to look at 30 days. Some people only want to look at something less, five, 10 days, whatever. This is kind of how I've set it up. And then I have a three, seven and 14 day breakdown. I'm looking at trends. So here, for example, return on ad spend, I can see by the color coding, which is conditional formatting, which is here, I can see based on the color scale, the good and bad days. But sometimes when you're looking at day to day stats, you can oversee the impact over time. So for example, I might look at today's 146 and kind of look at that and say, you know, that's not so good, for example. And you know, this is just early day spend, but I might look at that and say, you know, maybe it's not so good. But I'm looking at the trend as well. And sometimes, for example, I'll know that for example, this time of day, 146 rise is okay. Later on in the day, it's not. So if that's the case, and you know, media buyers coming in, they would go and do the analysis and drill into the ad account itself and find out what's working, what's not, for example, there as well. But at least on the on the trends, I can see, for example, Ryus has really picked up recently. So we've actually increased our spend. And we've increased AOV at the same time. And, you know, we're profiting much higher. So there's a few initiatives that we put in that have had a big impact. But the overall, Rowe S is over the 14 days really good as well. One thing that I add is also to add the daily targets. Now this helps, because we have multiple media buyers in particular, looking over multiple ad accounts, you won't always remember what the targets are. So obviously, if you're just one person running one ad account, you'll know what your targets are. Me, for example, if I'm looking at any given ad account, I can't always remember what the targets are. So we've added this row here, just to give an idea of what the baselines and benchmarks are. So for example, our target spend is 5k a day, target row as as a minimum is 250% AOV 90 etc, etc, all the way through. So then I can look at that versus where we are right now to look at where we are on performance. So for example, if I scan down to the three seven 14 day view, we're we're above target. So I'm kind of happy with performance here. So when I'm spot checking accounts, I know where to focus my attention, for example, you know, media buys, etc. We're not wasting time trying to figure out what the targets are is this on track, etc, etc. We just know just looking at the data how it's going, the color coding through conditional formatting. What that does is allows me to scan and see how performance is. So what I actually do is when I do my conditional formatting, I select all of these columns, including the target, what that means is I can see based on the colors, what the performance

## [12:12 - 15:18]

is based on my benchmark. So this 250%, if for example, you know, all of the performance here is below 250, then I'll be able to recognize that because this 250 would actually be green. That gives me an indicator that if to if the 250% is the highest row as is my daily target of all of these, the rough track, when this is red, it means there are other values that are higher. So actually, we're on track. So this is a slightly different way of looking at it. And the same applies on these as well. And these are just rough indicators. Obviously, sometimes sales conversion rate will be higher or low and you still get good performance. As you can see here, conversion rate is 2.7. So it's below the 3%. AOV is much higher, because our cost per click is that much better and also our AOV. So when it comes to understanding the data, we've covered this already in the KPIs part of the training. So that's the kind of main top level KPIs that we're tracking. Then we have our secondary metrics. So here now, I'm looking at this is the typical kind of econ funnel. So view content, add to car, initiate, check out the three main metrics we're tracking. And again, just looking at the trends of the data, like when you're looking at data and analysis, trends are one of the most important parts of understanding what's working and what's not. Because that gives you an idea of, for example, today, if the add to car rate is $4, I can look at the trend and also the three seven day, 14 day zoomed out data and see how it compares. So I can quickly see obviously it's red, and $4 is much higher than our average. So there might be a problem there. So that just gives you an idea of where to focus on. So for example, here, yes, our ROAS is $146, CPA is $58. But the reason why is actually in the detail. So our add to car rate is higher, our initiate, check out rate is higher. And I can also go through further down the funnel and see what what the conversion rates are through the funnel. The first part of the funnel that I track is the landing page view conversion rate. This is slightly different to what you might expect in a funnel. Because what I'm doing here is tracking as a ratio, how many outbound clicks were generated, and how many of those generated a landing page view. This is automatically generated by Facebook based on the standard pixel event, which is page view. So if you've looked into pixel viewer in Chrome before, you'll you'll see a default kind of page view event that's fine. That relates to this. What that tells me is although 120 outbound clicks were generated from Facebook, only 106 of those actually fired that pixel. That could be for many reasons. Number one, it could be someone's clicked and didn't wait for the page to load, maybe the pixel didn't load, maybe there's cookie blocker. But also, it could be that there's bot traffic as well. So this is

## [15:18 - 18:22]

just something that I like to monitor to keep an eye of traffic quality. So for example, the benchmark just generally for any kind of website and ad account is 85%. If it's consistently lower than that, then there's a problem somewhere. So it could be cookie blockers, could be bot traffic. For example, when you run page post engagement campaigns, sometimes you'll see that the landing page view conversion rate from outbound clicks is even lower, like you're talking 30, 40, 50%, because sometimes a lot of that is bot traffic. And that's obviously useless to you as well. Then I kind of look down my funnel. So of those that clicked into the site, how many find the view content pixel of those that find the view content pixel, how many added to cart. So this is my product to cart conversion rate, for example, and I can track that all the way through. From those that were in the in the cart stage, how many went to checkout. So here you can see, for example, although these numbers are okay, my conversion from adding to cart to checkout is actually lower than it's been. That's a big explanation why our performance today is bad, for example, then I can track that all the way through. So those are initiate the checkout, how many went on to purchase. The reason I don't have payment info here is because we have a lot of PayPal sales and PayPal kind of takes people out that last payment page on this ecom store, for example, and on many as well. So I'm more kind of interested in these stats. And then this final column is for those that added to cart, how many then went on to buy. So these are different ways that I'm looking at and analyzing my metrics. And then these are just just the kind of hard numbers at the end of it as well. And of these, the main ones I'm looking at are CPM and frequency. So this ecom store right now, for example, is well primed for low CPMs, we're in a competitive space, it's the typical kind of female shopper space that's that attracts higher CPMs and higher competition. But we're able to keep that down through the exact strategies that we've talked about in the BPM method, which is auction control, page engagement and things like that, which we've spent many, many months on to get right. And that keeps our CPMs consistently low, such that even with our kind of click through rates, we can still generate really decent cost per clicks as well in a really competitive space. And you know, that just means that we've got so much more potential. So that's kind of how I use this spreadsheet to analyze, just to recap, make sure that this name here matches what's in raw data so that when you pull your data through, it matches up. If you don't have or don't want to use super metrics, you can still create this data via ads manager. It's just not something we do anymore, because it's so much faster and efficient to put it through super metrics. And like I say, just make sure that your data is split up, whether it's Legion or Ecom, this is just how

## [18:22 - 19:12]

we kind of prefer to analyze it. And you know, for as many ad accounts as you've got, you would then create multiple versions of this as well so that you can manage and monitor more than one if you want. So that's the main thing from this. Number one, make sure you get your raw data set up properly using super metrics. If you are using super metrics, and then in your dashboard, make sure you're setting the right ad account name, and then just let the data flow through and get used to seeing and understanding the data. So as you're working through your ad account, you're spending money, you're making sales, you're generating leads, etc. Get used to the data, and then you'll be able to make faster decisions when you're scanning your columns and your data as well.
